# How to Authenticate

Pass your API key correctly on every request.

Every request to the Widgets API must include a bearer token in the
`Authorization` header. This page explains how to obtain a key and attach
it to your requests. For the full auth scheme spec (scopes, error codes,
token expiry), see the [API Reference](/api-reference).

## Get an API key

Sign in to your dashboard and navigate to **Settings → API Keys**. Click
**Create key**, give it a name, and copy the value — it won't be shown again.

## Attach the token

Pass your key as a bearer token on every request:

```bash
curl https://api.example.com/v1/widgets \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
```

## Common errors

| Status | Code           | Cause                                       |
| ------ | -------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| 401    | `unauthorized` | Missing or malformed `Authorization` header |
| 401    | `unauthorized` | Key has been revoked                        |

If you receive a 401, double-check that the header value is exactly
`Bearer <key>` with no extra whitespace.

---

# Setting Up the API Reference

How to connect your own OpenAPI spec and configure the interactive API explorer.

The API Reference tab is powered by [Scalar](https://scalar.com), an open-source
interactive API explorer. Pepa ships with a working example spec built on
[JSONPlaceholder](https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com) — a free hosted service
with real GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE endpoints for posts, comments, todos,
and users. You can execute requests directly in the explorer with no API key.
This guide explains how to swap it out for your own spec.

---

## How it works

The pipeline has three parts:

<Steps>
  <Step title="Your spec lives in openapi/">
    Any `.yaml` or `.json` file you place in `apps/docs/openapi/` is your source
    of truth.
  </Step>
  <Step title="It gets copied to public/ at build time">
    The `copy-openapi` script (run automatically before `pnpm dev` and
    `pnpm build`) copies everything in `openapi/` to `public/openapi/`, where
    Next.js serves it as a static file.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Scalar loads it at runtime">
    The API reference page points Scalar at the served file. Scalar fetches it
    in the browser and renders the interactive explorer — no server required.
  </Step>
</Steps>

---

## Swap in your own spec

<Steps>
  <Step title="Add your spec file">
    Copy your OpenAPI 3.x spec into `apps/docs/openapi/`. The filename is up to
    you:

    ```
    apps/docs/openapi/
    ├── example-api.yaml   ← JSONPlaceholder demo (can delete this)
    └── your-api.yaml      ← your real spec
    ```

    <Callout type="tip">
      Both OpenAPI 3.0 and 3.1 work. JSON is also accepted alongside YAML.
    </Callout>
  </Step>

  <Step title="Point Scalar at your file">
    Open `apps/docs/app/api-reference/page.tsx` and update the `url` field:

    ```ts
    const instance = createApiReference(el, {
      url: "/openapi/your-api.yaml",   // ← change this
      darkMode: document.documentElement.dataset.theme === "dark",
    });
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Remove the example spec (optional)">
    Delete `apps/docs/openapi/example-api.yaml` once your spec is wired up.
    It's only there to give the tab something to render on a fresh clone.
  </Step>

  <Step title="Restart the dev server">
    ```bash
    pnpm dev
    ```

    The `predev` step runs `copy-openapi` automatically, so your spec is copied
    to `public/openapi/` before the server starts. Navigate to the API Reference
    tab to confirm Scalar is loading your spec.
  </Step>
</Steps>

---

## Disable the tab entirely

If your project doesn't have an API, turn the tab off in `pepa.config.ts`:

```ts
{ id: "api-reference", label: "API Reference", href: "/api-reference", enabled: false },
```

The tab disappears from the header. The route still exists but won't appear in
navigation or the search index.

---

## Multiple specs

If you have more than one API (for example, a public API and an internal one),
you can drop multiple files into `openapi/` and switch between them:

```
apps/docs/openapi/
├── public-api.yaml
└── internal-api.yaml
```

Point Scalar at whichever one you want as the default in `page.tsx`. To let
readers switch between specs, you'd need to extend the page component to render
a selector — this isn't built in yet, but the files are all served statically so
client-side switching is straightforward to add.

---

## Common issues

<Accordion>
  <AccordionItem title="The explorer shows 'Failed to fetch' or a network error">
    The spec file isn't being served. Check that:

    1. Your file is in `apps/docs/openapi/` (not `apps/docs/public/openapi/` directly)
    2. You restarted the dev server after adding the file (so `predev` ran `copy-openapi`)
    3. The filename in `page.tsx` matches the actual filename exactly, including extension
  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="Scalar loads but my spec has validation errors">
    Scalar renders even with spec errors, but some operations may not display
    correctly. Run your spec through the
    [Swagger Editor](https://editor.swagger.io) or the Scalar validator to catch
    issues before publishing.
  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="The API Reference tab is slow on first load">
    This is expected in dev — Turbopack compiles Scalar's JavaScript module on
    first visit (~5 seconds). After that first compile it's cached for the rest
    of the dev session. Production builds are pre-compiled and load instantly.
  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

---

# Components and Symbols

How to use shared UI components, reusable snippets, and text variables in MDX.

## UI components

Import from `docs-ui` in any MDX file:

```mdx
import { Callout, Tabs, Tab, CodeGroup, Kbd, Icon } from "docs-ui";
```

### Callout

```mdx
<Callout type="tip">Pro tip: do the thing.</Callout>
<Callout type="warning">This will overwrite existing data.</Callout>
```

Types: `tip`, `warning`, `note`, `danger`.

### Tabs and CodeGroup

````mdx
<Tabs>
  <Tab label="pnpm">```bash pnpm install ```</Tab>
  <Tab label="npm">```bash npm install ```</Tab>
</Tabs>
````

### Kbd

Inline keyboard shortcut display:

```mdx
Press <Kbd>⌘K</Kbd> to open search.
Press <Kbd>Ctrl</Kbd>+<Kbd>C</Kbd> to copy.
```

### Icon

Pass any [Lucide](https://lucide.dev/icons/) icon component:

```mdx
import { Icon } from "docs-ui";
import { ExternalLink, AlertTriangle, CheckCircle } from "lucide-react";

<Icon icon={ExternalLink} />
<Icon icon={AlertTriangle} size={20} />
```

Default size is 16px. Browse available icons at lucide.dev/icons.

---

## Text variables

Short strings that should be consistent across all docs — product name,
current version, URLs — live in `apps/docs/lib/vars.ts`:

```ts
export const vars = {
  productName: "Pepa",
  version: "1.0.0",
  repoUrl: "https://github.com/your-org/pepa",
};
```

Use them in MDX:

```mdx
import { vars } from "@/lib/vars";

**{vars.productName}** version {vars.version} introduced this feature.
```

To add a variable: add a key to `vars` in `lib/vars.ts`. It's immediately
available everywhere it's imported.

---

## Content snippets

Snippets are reusable content components — anything from a sentence to a full
code block — defined in `packages/ui/src/snippets/`. Edit the component once
and it updates in every doc that uses it.

```mdx
import { AuthNote, BearerExample } from "docs-ui/snippets";

<AuthNote />
<BearerExample />
```

### Adding a snippet

1. Create `packages/ui/src/snippets/MySnippet.tsx`:

```tsx
export function MySnippet() {
  return <p>Content that appears identically in every doc that uses it.</p>;
}
```

2. Export it from `packages/ui/src/snippets/index.tsx`:

```ts
export { MySnippet } from "./MySnippet";
```

3. Import and use it in any MDX file:

```mdx
import { MySnippet } from "docs-ui/snippets"
<MySnippet />
```

Snippets can contain anything a React component can: prose, code blocks,
callouts, tables, other components. Keep them focused — if a snippet grows
into a full page's worth of content, it should become a real doc instead.

---

# Feature Showcase

A living demo of every component and platform feature. Use this as a reference when authoring.

This page demonstrates every component available to doc authors.
The `status: beta` badge in the header above is showing off that feature.

No imports needed — all components are globally registered.

---

## Callouts

<Callout type="tip">
  Use `type="tip"` for helpful hints that aren't critical.
</Callout>

<Callout type="warning">
  Use `type="warning"` for things that can go wrong.
</Callout>

## Tabs

<Tabs>
  <Tab label="pnpm">```bash pnpm install && pnpm dev ```</Tab>
  <Tab label="npm">```bash npm install && npm run dev ```</Tab>
  <Tab label="yarn">```bash yarn && yarn dev ```</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Syntax-highlighted code

```typescript
async function fetchWidgets(apiKey: string) {
  const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets", {
    headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
  });
  return res.json();
}
```

## Tables

| Component  | MDX tag                      | What it does                   |
| ---------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Callout    | `<Callout type="tip">`       | Tips, warnings, notes          |
| Tabs / Tab | `<Tabs><Tab label="...">`    | Multi-tab content              |
| CodeGroup  | `<CodeGroup>`                | Grouped code blocks            |
| Kbd        | `<Kbd>⌘K</Kbd>`              | Keyboard shortcut styling      |
| Icon       | `<Icon icon={...} />`        | Inline Lucide icons            |
| Var        | `<Var name="productName" />` | Text variable                  |
| AuthNote   | `<AuthNote />`               | Reusable auth reminder snippet |

## Keyboard shortcuts

Press <Kbd>⌘K</Kbd> to open search.
Press <Kbd>⌘</Kbd>+<Kbd>Shift</Kbd>+<Kbd>P</Kbd> for the command palette.

## Icons

<LucideCircleCheck size={16} /> Success &nbsp;
<LucideTriangleAlert size={16} /> Warning &nbsp;
<LucideInfo size={16} /> Info &nbsp;
<LucideLink size={16} /> Link

Any icon from [lucide.dev/icons](https://lucide.dev/icons) is available as
`<Lucide{IconName} />` — no import required.

## Text variables

Product name: **<Var name="productName" />** · Version: **<Var name="version" />**

Defined in `lib/vars.ts` — change once, updates everywhere.

## Reusable snippet

`<AuthNote />` is defined once in `packages/ui/src/snippets/AuthNote.tsx`:

<AuthNote />

## Status badges

Add to any page's frontmatter — stable pages get no badge:

```yaml
status: beta          # blue
status: experimental  # orange
status: deprecated    # red
```

## Steps

Numbered steps with a connecting line. Code blocks, callouts, and any other
content can live inside a step.

<Steps>
  <Step title="Install dependencies">
    ```bash
    pnpm install
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Configure your project">
    Edit `apps/docs/pepa.config.ts` to set your project name and enable the
    sections you need.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Add your first doc">
    Create an `.mdx` file under `content/docs/` with the required frontmatter,
    then run `pnpm dev` to see it appear in the sidebar.

    ```yaml
    ---
    title: My First Page
    nav: Tutorials
    order: 1
    ---
    ```

  </Step>
  <Step title="Deploy">
    Push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds the static export and publishes it
    to GitHub Pages automatically.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Tiles

Cards arranged in a responsive grid. A single tile expands to fill the full
width automatically.

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="Tutorials" iconName="BookOpen" href="/getting-started">
    Learning-oriented guides that walk you through a first success.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="API Reference" iconName="Code" href="/api-reference">
    Auto-generated from your OpenAPI spec via Scalar.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Cookbook" iconName="ChefHat" href="/cookbook/overview">
    Copy-pasteable recipes — minimal explanation, maximum code.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Contributors" iconName="Users" href="/contributors/overview">
    Platform docs for maintainers: sweeps, style linting, and components.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

A single tile fills the full row:

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="Single tile" iconName="Zap" href="/getting-started">
    One tile always expands to the full content width.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

## Accordion

Collapsible sections. Uses native `<details>` — no JavaScript required.

<Accordion>
  <AccordionItem title="What is Diátaxis?">
    Diátaxis is a framework that organises documentation into four types:
    **Tutorials**, **How-to Guides**, **Reference**, and **Explanation**.
    Each type serves a different user need.
  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="Do I need to use all four types?" open>
    No. All four Diátaxis sections (and the special types like Cookbook) are
    **opt-in**. Set `enabled: false` in `pepa.config.ts` to hide any section
    you don't need.
  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="Can I nest pages in the sidebar?">
    Yes — add a `navParent` field to your frontmatter to group related pages
    under a collapsible heading within a nav group:

    ```yaml
    nav: Reference
    navParent: Authentication
    ```

  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

## Nested nav sidebar

Pages can be grouped under a collapsible sub-section within any nav group.
Add `navParent` to the page's frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: Token Types
nav: Reference
navParent: Authentication
order: 1
---
```

The sidebar renders the sub-section collapsed by default. It auto-opens when
the active page lives inside it.

## Heading anchor links

Hover any heading — a chain-link icon appears. Click to copy the direct URL.

---

# Frontmatter Reference

Every frontmatter field, what it does, and when to use it.

Every `.mdx` file in `content/docs/` must have a frontmatter block. Fields
are validated at build time — an invalid or missing required field fails the
build with a clear error message pointing at the file.

## Required fields

### `title`

```yaml
title: How to Paginate Results
```

Shown as the page `<h1>`, in the browser tab, and in search results.
Do not repeat it as a heading inside the page body.

## Optional fields

### `description`

```yaml
description: Step-by-step guide to cursor-based pagination in the Widgets API.
```

One sentence. Shown in gray under the title and used as the meta description
for search engines.

### `type`

```yaml
type: how-to
```

The Diátaxis content type. Drives rendering hints and helps readers know what
to expect before they start reading.

| Value | When to use |
|-------|-------------|
| `tutorial` | Teaching something — reader is learning |
| `how-to` | Solving a specific problem — reader has a goal |
| `reference` | Factual lookup — reader knows what they want |
| `explanation` | Background understanding — reader wants context |
| `quickstart` | Fastest path to a working result |
| `changelog` | Release notes |
| `faq` | Question-and-answer format |
| `glossary` | Term definitions |
| `cookbook` | Copy-pasteable recipes, minimal prose |
| `architecture` | How the system itself is built (contributor-facing) |
| `migration` | Version upgrade instructions |

### `section`

```yaml
section: docs
```

Which top-level tab this page belongs to. Must match a section `id` in
`pepa.config.ts`.

| Value | Tab |
|-------|-----|
| `docs` | Main docs tab (default) |
| `api-reference` | API Reference tab |
| `cookbook` | Cookbook tab |
| `contributors` | Contributors tab |

### `nav`

```yaml
nav: How-to Guides
```

The sidebar group label within the section. Valid values:

`Tutorials` · `How-to Guides` · `Reference` · `Explanation` ·
`Quickstart` · `Changelog` · `FAQ` · `Glossary`

### `order`

```yaml
order: 3
```

Integer. Position within the sidebar group — lower numbers sort first.
Pages with the same `order` sort alphabetically by title.

### `status`

```yaml
status: beta
```

Adds a colored badge next to the page title. Omit this field for stable pages —
stable is the default and gets no badge.

| Value | Badge color | When to use |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| `beta` | Blue | Feature works but the API or content may change |
| `experimental` | Orange | May change significantly or be removed |
| `deprecated` | Red | Will be removed; link to the replacement |

### `navParent`

```yaml
navParent: Authentication
```

Creates a collapsible sub-section within the sidebar group. All pages that share
the same `nav` and `navParent` values are grouped under a twirl-open heading.
The sub-section opens automatically when the reader is on one of its pages.

```yaml
# Example: two pages nested under "Authentication" inside the Reference group
nav: Reference
navParent: Authentication
```

Omit this field to have the page appear directly in the top level of its `nav`
group with no nesting.

### `hidden`

```yaml
hidden: true
```

Excludes the page from the sidebar and nav tree. The page still exists at its
URL and is included in the build — useful for landing pages or pages linked
from elsewhere but not meant to appear in navigation.

### `copyable`

```yaml
copyable: true
```

Shows a **Copy page** button in the top-right corner of the content header.
Clicking it copies the full rendered text of the page to the clipboard — useful
for readers who want to paste the content into an AI assistant, a notes app, or
another document.

The button is off by default. Add `copyable: true` to any page where sharing
the full content in one click would be genuinely useful — long reference pages,
tutorials, and cookbook recipes are good candidates. Avoid it on pages that are
mostly navigation or placeholder content.

<Callout type="tip">
  The copy captures the rendered text (what readers see), not the raw MDX
  source. Code blocks, table content, and component output are all included.
</Callout>

---

# Contributor Overview

How this documentation platform works and where everything lives.

This tab is for people maintaining the docs, not for end users of the product.
It covers the platform's own mechanics: how content is structured, what tools
are available, and how to use them.

## Repo layout

```
apps/docs/
  content/docs/      MDX source files — this is where you write
  components/        React components (layout, sidebar, TOC, MDX renderer)
  lib/               nav.ts, vars.ts — shared logic and text variables
  scripts/           CLI tools: find-replace, search index, llms.txt
  openapi/           OpenAPI spec source (copied to public/ at build time)
  pepa.config.ts     Enable/disable top-level nav tabs and features
  content-collections.ts  Frontmatter schema — build fails on invalid fields

packages/ui/src/
  Callout.tsx, Tabs.tsx, CodeGroup.tsx, Kbd.tsx, Icon.tsx
  snippets/          Reusable content blocks (edit once, updates everywhere)
```

## How the nav is built

There is no nav config file. The sidebar is derived entirely from frontmatter:

- `section` determines which top-level tab the page appears under
- `nav` determines which sidebar group it belongs to within that tab
- `order` sets the position within the group

Adding a page to the right place in the nav is just a matter of setting
the correct `section`, `nav`, and `order` in its frontmatter.

## How builds work

```bash
pnpm dev      # starts Next.js dev server at localhost:3000
pnpm validate # check everything without building (see below)
pnpm build    # full static export into out/
```

The prebuild step runs automatically before `pnpm build`:

1. Copies `openapi/` → `public/openapi/` (so the spec is served statically)
2. Runs content-collections (validates frontmatter, compiles MDX)
3. Generates `public/search-index.json`
4. Generates `public/llms.txt` and `public/llms-full.txt` for AI agents

A missing required frontmatter field (like `title`) fails the build
immediately with a clear error — that's intentional.

## Validating without building

`pnpm validate` runs three fast checks and exits with a clear pass/fail:

```bash
✓ Frontmatter & MDX    (356ms)  — schema errors, MDX parse failures
✓ TypeScript           (2s)     — type errors across the whole app
✓ Internal links       (0 broken) — /slug links that don't point to a real page
```

Run it before opening a PR. CI runs it automatically on every push and pull
request. If a check fails, the output tells you the file and line number:

```
✗ Internal links     (1 broken)

  content/docs/cookbook/overview.mdx
    line 26  →  /cookbook/handle-errors  (no matching page)
```

External links (http/https URLs) are checked separately by
[lychee](https://lychee.cli.rs) in CI after the build step.

## What the platform adds to every page

Breadcrumbs, word count, previous/next navigation, and the "Edit on GitHub" link are all generated
automatically — no frontmatter required. See [Platform Features](/contributors/platform-features)
for how each one works and how to configure them.

---

# Platform Features

What Pepa adds to every page automatically, and how to configure it.

Pepa adds several UI features to every doc page. Most of them are automatic — they derive their data from
the frontmatter you're already writing. A few are configured at the platform level in `pepa.config.ts`.

## Breadcrumbs

Every page shows a breadcrumb trail above the title:

```
Docs / Quickstart
```

The first segment is the section label (e.g., "Docs", "Cookbook") and links back to the section's
landing page. The second segment is the `nav` group the page belongs to. These come from the `section`
and `nav` frontmatter fields — no extra work required.

## Word count and reading time

A metadata line appears between the description and the page body:

```
1,234 words · 6 min read
```

Both are computed automatically from the page content:

- **Word count** is derived from the prose text, with code blocks and JSX component tags stripped out.
  It reflects the amount of content a reader will encounter, not the raw source size.
- **Reading time** uses 200 words per minute as a baseline. It's shown as a secondary signal — useful
  for planning, but reading speed varies widely between readers.

Neither field appears in frontmatter. They update automatically as pages are edited.

## Previous and Next navigation

At the bottom of every page, Pepa shows cards linking to the previous and next page in the section:

```
← Getting Started         API Authentication →
```

Pages are ordered by nav group (in the order listed in the frontmatter `nav` enum), then by `order`
within each group, then alphabetically by title. Pages with `hidden: true` are excluded.

The order is the same as the sidebar, so "previous" and "next" always follow the reading path a
new reader would naturally take.

## Edit on GitHub

A small "Edit this page on GitHub" link appears below the previous/next cards. Clicking it opens
the source file for the current page on GitHub so readers can suggest corrections.

Configure it in `pepa.config.ts`:

```ts
features: {
  editOnGitHub: "https://github.com/your-org/your-repo/blob/main/apps/docs/content/docs",
}
```

Set `editOnGitHub` to the URL of the `content/docs` directory on GitHub, using the `blob/<branch>/`
path (not `tree/`). Pepa appends the page's slug and `.mdx` extension automatically.

To hide the link entirely — for example on a private or internal deployment — set the value to
`undefined` or remove the field:

```ts
features: {
  editOnGitHub: undefined,
}
```

## Copy page button

The copy-page button appears in the top-right corner of the content header, but only on pages where
you've explicitly opted in via frontmatter:

```yaml
copyable: true
```

See the [frontmatter reference](/contributors/frontmatter-reference) for details.

---

# Style Linting

How to run the Google Developer Style Guide linter and interpret its output.

Pepa uses [Vale](https://vale.sh) with the
[Google Developer Style Guide](https://developers.google.com/style) ruleset
to catch common style violations before they reach production.

## First-time setup

```bash
brew install vale      # install the Vale binary (once per machine)
pnpm vale:sync         # download the Google style rules (once per machine)
```

`vale:sync` downloads rules into `.vale/styles/` at the repo root.
That directory is gitignored — run `vale:sync` once after cloning.

## Running the linter

From the repo root:

```bash
pnpm lint:style                                              # all docs
pnpm lint:style apps/docs/content/docs/getting-started.mdx  # single file
```

## Reading the output

```
apps/docs/content/docs/getting-started.mdx
 14:12  warning  Use 'select' instead of 'click on'.  Google.We
 27:4   warning  Avoid using 'simply'.                Google.Simplicity
```

Each line shows: `line:col  severity  message  rule-name`.

Severity levels (configured in `.vale.ini`):

| Level | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `suggestion` | Worth considering; low urgency |
| `warning` | Likely a style issue; fix before merging |
| `error` | Must fix |

The current config (`MinAlertLevel = suggestion`) shows all levels.

## Common violations

| Vale says | Fix |
|-----------|-----|
| "click on" | Use "click" |
| "simply", "easy", "just" | Remove — they're dismissive |
| Passive voice detected | Rewrite to active voice |
| Heading not in sentence case | Capitalize only the first word and proper nouns |
| Oxford comma missing | Add the comma before "and" in a list |
| Future tense ("will") | Use present tense where possible |

## Adjusting the rules

The config lives at `.vale.ini` in the repo root. To raise the minimum
severity to warnings only:

```ini
MinAlertLevel = warning
```

To disable a specific rule for a file, add a comment:

```mdx
<!-- vale Google.Simplicity = NO -->
This is simply unavoidable.
<!-- vale Google.Simplicity = YES -->
```

---

# Running a Term Sweep

Find and interactively replace a term across all docs in one pass.

Use the sweep tool when a term, product name, or phrase needs to be updated
across multiple docs at once. It shows every match in context and lets you
decide per-match whether to accept the change.

## Basic usage

From `apps/docs/`:

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term"
```

This opens an interactive session. For each match you'll see:

```
content/docs/getting-started.mdx — match 1 of 4
   3 │
   4 │ This guide uses old term extensively.
   5 │

  - This guide uses old term extensively.
  + This guide uses new term extensively.

Accept? [y]es / [n]o / [a]ll / [q]uit:
```

## Keys

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `y` | Accept this match, move to the next |
| `n` | Skip this match, leave it unchanged |
| `a` | Accept this match and all remaining matches |
| `q` | Quit immediately, writing only the matches accepted so far |

## Preview without changing anything

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term" --dry-run
```

Prints every match with context. Writes nothing. Useful for auditing scope
before committing to a sweep.

## Accept all without reviewing

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term" --write
```

Accepts every match without prompting. Use this when you're confident the
substitution is always correct (e.g. a product rename with no ambiguous cases).

## Regex mode

```bash
pnpm find-replace "v(\d+\.\d+)" "version $1" --regex
```

Treats the first argument as a JavaScript regex. Capture groups are available
in the replacement as `$1`, `$2`, etc. Combine with `--write` or `--dry-run`
as needed.

## Scope

The sweep only touches `.mdx` files under `content/docs/`. It never modifies
app code, scripts, or configuration files.

---

# Authenticate a request

Add a bearer token to every API request.

**Goal:** Make an authenticated request to the Widgets API.

## The code

```bash
curl https://api.example.com/v1/widgets \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
```

```js
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets", {
  headers: {
    Authorization: `Bearer ${process.env.API_KEY}`,
  },
});
const { data } = await res.json();
```

```python
import os, httpx

resp = httpx.get(
    "https://api.example.com/v1/widgets",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['API_KEY']}"},
)
resp.raise_for_status()
data = resp.json()["data"]
```

## What to change

- Replace `YOUR_API_KEY` / `process.env.API_KEY` / `os.environ['API_KEY']`
  with however your app loads secrets.
- Replace the URL with your real API base URL.

---

# Handle errors

Branch on HTTP status codes and surface error messages from API responses.

## Goal

Detect failure responses, extract the error detail, and handle specific status
codes differently — without crashing or silently swallowing failures.

## The code

```typescript
async function apiFetch(path: string, options?: RequestInit) {
  const res = await fetch(`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com${path}`, options);

  if (!res.ok) {
    let message = `Request failed: ${res.status} ${res.statusText}`;

    // Try to extract a message from the response body
    try {
      const body = await res.json();
      if (body?.message) message = body.message;
      else if (body?.error) message = body.error;
    } catch {
      // Body wasn't JSON — use the status text
    }

    if (res.status === 401) throw new Error(`Unauthorized: ${message}`);
    if (res.status === 404) throw new Error(`Not found: ${message}`);
    if (res.status === 429) throw new Error(`Rate limited — retry after ${res.headers.get("Retry-After")}s`);
    if (res.status >= 500) throw new Error(`Server error: ${message}`);

    throw new Error(message);
  }

  // 204 No Content has no body
  if (res.status === 204) return null;

  return res.json();
}

// Usage
try {
  const post = await apiFetch("/posts/999"); // 404 — this ID doesn't exist
  console.log(post);
} catch (err) {
  console.error(err.message); // "Not found: {}"
}
```

## What to change

| Line | What to swap |
|------|-------------|
| `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com` | Your API base URL |
| `body?.message` / `body?.error` | The field name your API uses for error details |
| The `401` / `404` / `429` branches | Add or remove cases for your API's error codes |

---

# Cookbook

Copy-pasteable recipes for common tasks. Minimal explanation, maximum code.

Recipes are short, complete, and runnable. Each one solves exactly one problem.
For background on *why* something works the way it does, see the main docs.

<Callout type="tip">
  Every recipe uses the [JSONPlaceholder](https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com)
  API as the example. Swap in your own endpoints and the patterns transfer directly.
</Callout>

## What's here

| Recipe | What it covers |
|--------|---------------|
| [Authenticate a request](/cookbook/authenticate-a-request) | Add a bearer token to every request |
| [Paginate through results](/cookbook/paginate-results) | Walk a cursor-paginated list endpoint |
| [Handle errors](/cookbook/handle-errors) | Branch on status codes, surface error messages |

## How recipes are structured

Each recipe has:

1. **The goal** — one sentence
2. **The code** — complete, copy-pasteable
3. **What to change** — the two or three lines you'll need to adapt

Nothing else. If you want context, the main docs have it.

---

# Paginate through results

Walk a cursor-paginated list endpoint until all records are fetched.

**Goal:** Fetch every widget across multiple pages using cursor-based pagination.

## The code

```js
async function fetchAllWidgets(apiKey) {
  const widgets = [];
  let cursor = null;

  do {
    const url = new URL("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets");
    url.searchParams.set("limit", "100");
    if (cursor) url.searchParams.set("cursor", cursor);

    const res = await fetch(url, {
      headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
    });
    const body = await res.json();

    widgets.push(...body.data);
    cursor = body.next_cursor;
  } while (cursor);

  return widgets;
}
```

```python
import httpx

def fetch_all_widgets(api_key: str) -> list:
    widgets, cursor = [], None

    while True:
        params = {"limit": 100}
        if cursor:
            params["cursor"] = cursor

        resp = httpx.get(
            "https://api.example.com/v1/widgets",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"},
            params=params,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        body = resp.json()

        widgets.extend(body["data"])
        cursor = body.get("next_cursor")
        if not cursor:
            break

    return widgets
```

## What to change

- `limit` — max is 100; lower it if responses are slow or payloads are large.
- The `next_cursor` field name — check your API's response schema if it differs.

---

# Getting Started

Get Pepa running and publish your first doc in under ten minutes.

**Pepa** is an open-source documentation platform you own completely. Clone it,
fill it with your content, and ship it — no vendor lock-in, no monthly bill, no
login required to read your own docs.

This guide walks you through setup from zero to a live site with your first
custom page.

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="Write in MDX" iconName="FileText">
    Markdown plus React components. If you know Markdown, you already know 80%
    of what you need.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Nav builds itself" iconName="Layers">
    No nav config files. The sidebar is generated from your file's frontmatter.
    Add a file, set three fields, done.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Deploy anywhere" iconName="Globe">
    Pepa exports to a folder of static HTML. It runs on GitHub Pages, Netlify,
    Vercel, S3 — anything that can serve files.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

---

## Before you begin

You'll need three things installed on your machine:

<Accordion>
  <AccordionItem title="Node.js 20 or later">
    Check your version with `node --version`. If you need to install or upgrade,
    download from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org) or use a version manager like
    `nvm` or `fnm`.
  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="pnpm">
    Pepa uses pnpm as its package manager. If you have Node installed, you
    already have `corepack` — just run:

    ```bash
    corepack enable
    corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate
    ```

    Or install pnpm directly: `npm install -g pnpm`

  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="Git">
    Check with `git --version`. Git ships with macOS and most Linux distros.
    Windows users can get it from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com).
  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

---

## Set up Pepa

<Steps>
  <Step title="Clone the repository">
    Replace `my-docs` with whatever you want to call your project:

    ```bash
    git clone https://github.com/your-org/pepa my-docs
    cd my-docs
    ```

    <Callout type="tip">
      Want to push your docs to your own GitHub repo later? Delete the `.git`
      folder and run `git init` to start fresh with your own history.
    </Callout>

  </Step>

  <Step title="Install dependencies">
    From inside the `my-docs` folder:

    ```bash
    pnpm install
    ```

    This installs everything in one shot — the Next.js app, the shared UI
    package, and all tooling.

  </Step>

  <Step title="Start the dev server">
    ```bash
    cd apps/docs
    pnpm dev
    ```

    Open [localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000). You should see this page.
    Every change you make to an MDX file hot-reloads in the browser instantly.

  </Step>

  <Step title="Make it yours">
    Open `apps/docs/pepa.config.ts`. This is the one file that controls your
    site's name and which top-level nav tabs are visible:

    ```ts
    const config: PepaConfig = {
      name: "my-docs",          // ← change this to your project name
      sections: [
        { id: "docs",          label: "Docs",          href: "/getting-started", enabled: true },
        { id: "api-reference", label: "API Reference", href: "/api-reference",   enabled: false }, // ← enable when ready
        { id: "cookbook",      label: "Cookbook",      href: "/cookbook/overview", enabled: false },
      ],
      // ...
    }
    ```

    Save the file. The header updates immediately.

  </Step>

  <Step title="Write your first doc">
    Create a new file anywhere under `apps/docs/content/docs/`. The file path
    becomes the URL. For example:

    ```
    content/docs/introduction.mdx  →  localhost:3000/introduction
    content/docs/api/auth.mdx      →  localhost:3000/api/auth
    ```

    Paste this starter frontmatter and save:

    ```yaml
    ---
    title: My First Page
    description: A short description shown under the title.
    type: tutorial
    section: docs
    nav: Tutorials
    order: 1
    ---

    Hello, world! This is my first Pepa page.
    ```

    Your page appears in the sidebar immediately — no restart needed.

  </Step>
</Steps>

---

## How the sidebar works

There is no nav config file. The sidebar is generated entirely from your
frontmatter fields:

| Field          | What it does                                                                                   |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `section`      | Which top-level tab this page belongs to (`docs`, `api-reference`, `cookbook`, `contributors`) |
| `nav`          | The sidebar group label within that section (`Tutorials`, `Reference`, `How-to Guides`, etc.)  |
| `order`        | Position within the group — lower numbers appear first                                         |
| `navParent`    | Optional sub-section within a group, creates a collapsible nested group                        |
| `hidden: true` | Keeps the page out of the sidebar (useful for landing pages)                                   |

A page with `nav: Reference` and `order: 2` will appear as the second item in
the "Reference" group. That's it — no other config needed.

<Callout type="tip">
  To create a nested sub-section, add `navParent: "Authentication"` to any
  page's frontmatter. It groups under a collapsible "Authentication" heading
  inside whatever `nav` group the page belongs to.
</Callout>

---

## How the project is organized

```
pepa/
├── apps/
│   └── docs/
│       ├── content/docs/      ← Your MDX files live here
│       ├── app/               ← Next.js App Router pages and layouts
│       ├── components/        ← Sidebar, TOC, MDX renderer, top tabs
│       ├── lib/               ← nav.ts (builds the sidebar), vars.ts (text variables)
│       ├── scripts/           ← CLI tools: search index, find-replace sweep, llms.txt
│       ├── openapi/           ← Your OpenAPI spec (copied to public/ at build time)
│       └── pepa.config.ts     ← Site name, enabled sections, feature flags
│
└── packages/
    └── ui/
        └── src/               ← Shared components: Callout, Tabs, Steps, Tiles, etc.
            └── snippets/      ← Reusable content blocks (edit once, updates everywhere)
```

The only folder you'll spend most of your time in is `content/docs/`. Everything
else can stay as-is until you want to customize deeper.

---

## Writing in MDX

MDX is Markdown with superpowers. Everything you already know from Markdown works
exactly the same — headings, bold, lists, code blocks, links. MDX just lets you
drop in React components when you need richer content.

<Accordion>
  <AccordionItem title="What does an MDX file look like?" open>
    ````mdx
    ---
    title: How to Authenticate
    description: Add bearer token auth to your API requests.
    type: how-to
    section: docs
    nav: How-to Guides
    order: 1
    ---

    ## Get your API key

    Go to your dashboard and copy your key.

    <Callout type="warning">
      Never commit your API key to version control.
    </Callout>

    ## Make your first request

    ```bash
    curl https://api.example.com/v1/widgets \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
    ```
    ````

  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="Which components can I use?">
    All components are globally available — no import statements needed. Just use
    them directly:

    | Component | What it's for |
    |-----------|--------------|
    | `<Callout type="tip">` | Tips, warnings, notes, danger alerts |
    | `<Steps><Step title="...">` | Numbered step sequences |
    | `<Tabs><Tab label="...">` | Multi-tab content (great for multi-language code) |
    | `<Tiles><Tile title="...">` | Card grids for navigation or feature overviews |
    | `<Accordion><AccordionItem title="...">` | Collapsible sections |
    | `<Kbd>⌘K</Kbd>` | Keyboard shortcut styling |
    | `<Var name="productName" />` | Text variables (edit in `lib/vars.ts`) |

    See every component in action on the
    [Feature Showcase](/contributors/feature-showcase) page.

  </AccordionItem>
  <AccordionItem title="What's the difference between all the `type` values?">
    The `type` field follows the [Diátaxis](https://diataxis.fr) framework — a
    way of thinking about what kind of content a page is:

    - **tutorial** — Learning-oriented. Teaches by doing. ("Build your first X")
    - **how-to** — Task-oriented. Solves a specific problem. ("How to do X")
    - **reference** — Information-oriented. Facts and specs. ("The X field accepts…")
    - **explanation** — Understanding-oriented. Answers "why". ("Why Pepa works this way")

    You can also use `quickstart`, `cookbook`, `faq`, `glossary`, `changelog`,
    or `migration` for special content types. The `type` value doesn't change
    how the page renders — it's there to help you think clearly about what
    you're writing.

  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

---

## Build and deploy

When you're ready to publish:

```bash
pnpm build
```

This runs the full pipeline: validates all frontmatter, compiles MDX, generates
the search index, and exports a static site into `apps/docs/out/`. Upload that
folder to any static host.

<Callout type="note">
  If you're deploying to GitHub Pages at a path like
  `https://you.github.io/my-docs/`, uncomment the `basePath` line in
  `apps/docs/next.config.ts` and set it to `/my-docs`.
</Callout>

---

## Why the schema is your friend

Every MDX file is validated against a schema before the build runs. If you
delete a required field or typo a value, the build fails immediately with a clear
error pointing at the exact file — instead of silently shipping a broken page.

Try it: delete the `title` field at the top of this file and save. Watch the
terminal. Then add it back.

<Callout type="tip">
  **You're editing a live example.** This page is at
  `apps/docs/content/docs/getting-started.mdx`. Saving any change here
  hot-reloads in the browser and re-validates against the schema. It's a safe
  place to experiment.
</Callout>

---

## Where to go next

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="API Reference" iconName="Code" href="/api-reference">
    Wire up your OpenAPI spec and get an auto-generated, interactive API
    explorer.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Cookbook" iconName="ChefHat" href="/cookbook/overview">
    Short, copy-pasteable recipes for common documentation patterns.
  </Tile>
  <Tile
    title="Components & Symbols"
    iconName="Layers"
    href="/contributors/components-and-symbols"
  >
    Full reference for every component, snippet, and text variable available in
    MDX.
  </Tile>
  <Tile
    title="Feature Showcase"
    iconName="Sparkles"
    href="/contributors/feature-showcase"
  >
    A single page demoing every component side-by-side. Good for copy-pasting.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

---

# Reference example

example document

## Example

Example info
